1999-01-01.title: Honduras Retreats From Estimate of Storm Deaths
1999-01-01.abstract: Two months after a hurricane  mauled this country, it is still unclear how many Hondurans died in the storm, but the Government has been forced to retreat from its earlier conclusion that at least 7,000 people perished.    Officials here acknowledge that their initial death tolls, gathered from panicked local officials in the chaotic days just after the storm, were riddled with inaccuracies. In some cases, local officials assumed that hundreds had died because entire neighborhoods had been destroyed, but later learned that the vast majority of villagers in those places had found higher ground and had survived.

1999-01-01.title: World Briefing
1999-01-01.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: 84% INFLATION -- Russian inflation hit 84 percent for 1998, the Government said. Russia suffered from hyperinflation after it moved to a market economy in 1992, but the Government seemed to be taming it until the financial crisis struck last summer. The economy contracted about 5 percent in 1998, leaving Russia trapped in one of the worst depressions experienced by an industrialized nation.   (AP)    SWITZERLAND: TALKS ON GERM WEAPONS -- Arms control experts from about 50 countries will resume negotiations in Geneva on Monday to strengthen checks on biological weapons, the United Nations said. In the three-week talks, the first of five sessions scheduled in 1999, the experts aim to make progress on a protocol to detect cheating on the 1972 Biological Weapons Convention. Western diplomats suspect at least 12 rogue states, including Iraq, of having biological weapons programs.   (Reuters)

1999-01-01.title: For Rebels In Congo, Popularity Is Elusive
1999-01-01.abstract: This city at a bend in the Congo River will soon become the new rebel headquarters, in a move that concedes a reality no one bothers to hide anymore: the rebellion may control ever-growing chunks of Congo, but it remains deeply unpopular with the people.    It is not hard to see why. The other evening Sadiki Tabou, 33, who washes cars for a living and does not make much anyway, was reduced to planting a table-sized plot in an incongruous place: next to Kisangani's port, which has been closed since the rebellion began in August and is thus empty of the flour, dried fish, salt and oil that normally float in huge barges up the river.

1999-01-01.title: Afghans and the U.N. Agencies Are Far Apart on Resuming Aid
1999-01-01.abstract: A hardening of positions by the United Nations and the movement that controls most of Afghanistan is crippling efforts to resume international relief work there, officials from both sides say.     Stalled talks with the Taliban, the militant Islamic movement that rules more than 90 percent of the country, mean that almost no significant work in vital areas like health and reconstruction from more than 20 years of civil war can take place in the country, which has now fallen under the grip of winter.

1999-01-01.title: Two Khmer Rouge Leaders Spend Beach Holiday in Shadow of Past
1999-01-01.abstract: Liberated at last from a life of revolution, two Khmer Rouge leaders came to the beach today with their families, their leisure wear and their sunglasses.     But their holiday was a drab one, spent mostly behind the dirty brown curtains of their tiny hotel rooms, trapped inside by their fear of a small group of reporters.

1999-01-02.title: Banks and Brokers Gear Up for the Euro
1999-01-02.abstract: A day after 11 European countries christened the euro as their new currency, financial institutions across the Continent began the enormous job of reprogramming their electronic networks before markets reopen Monday. Although most institutions are bracing for technical glitches, financial executives say they are cautiously optimistic about a smooth start. And investment experts predict the euro will boost the popularity of European stocks and bonds.    Article, page A5.

1999-01-02.title: Euro Expected to Unleash Bull Market
1999-01-02.abstract: Euroka! That's the view of many investors looking forward to the debut in major financial markets on Monday of the euro, the single currency that now binds 11 European countries and promises to create a more unified economy in much of Europe.    Europe, which has emerged in the past couple of years from a long slump, should become an increasingly popular place to buy stocks and bonds, experts say. ''The introduction of the euro will raise the profile of the region,'' said Erik B. Granade, an international portfolio manager for Invesco Capital Management in Atlanta.

1999-01-02.title: Beijing Journal; Writer Rides Dirty Laundry to the Top in China
1999-01-02.abstract: Each day An Dun receives a dozen calls from people desperate to spill their deepest secrets, vying to have their private lives laid bare and dissected in her popular weekly newspaper column. Some have become pregnant out of wedlock. Some have been raped by their bosses. Some have been abandoned by their husbands. Others have done the ditching themselves.    In conservative Chinese society, such topics are generally taboo even among close relatives and best friends, and they are certainly not mainstream media fare. But since 1996, when Ms. An started recording matter-of-factly the stories of real people caught in such predicaments, she has wowed the younger generation with her candor and helped to liberate a nation's skeletons from the closet.

1999-01-02.title: Looking Back at 20th Century, Pope Says Respecting Human Dignity Is Key to World Peace
1999-01-02.abstract: Pope John Paul II declared today that the defense of human dignity forms the foundation of world peace, and he urged the world to renew its dedication to the respect of individual human dignity at the end of a century often marked by its violation in war and violence.    Celebrating Mass in St. Peter's Basilica to mark World Peace Day, which was established by Pope Paul VI in 1967, John Paul invited his listeners to glance back over the past century. He evoked poignant images of ''two world wars, cemeteries, tombs to the fallen, destroyed families, tears and desperation, misery and suffering.''

1999-01-02.title: CAMBODIAN DENIES HE OPPOSED TRIAL FOR KHMER ROUGE
1999-01-02.abstract: In an angry statement, Prime Minister Hun Sen denied today that he opposed a trial for two high-ranking Khmer Rouge defectors and said he supported an investigation into the mass killings of the 1970's when the movement controlled Cambodia.    Responding to criticism for his statement this week that the defectors should be met with bouquets rather than handcuffs, Mr. Hun Sen said his first priority had been to secure peace and that the question of trials was the next order of business.

1999-01-03.title: North Korea's Hardening Line on U.S. Raises Fear of New Crisis
1999-01-03.abstract: Some experts fear that US faces a major confrontation with North Korea sometime this year as a result of North Korea's missile tests and suspicions that it is secretly developing nuclear weapons in underground complexes around the country (M)

1999-01-03.title: The Long, Erratic Arm of Justice
1999-01-03.abstract: Seth Mydans analysis of inequities hobbling Cambodia's development into a free and democratic state; contrasts warm official welcome given to Khieu Samphan and Nuon Chea, two Khmer Rouge leaders accused of killing more than a million people, with the imprisonment of Kim Sen and Meas Minea, two human rights workers who advised protesters who rallied recently against the dumping of toxic waste; says Cambodia remains a land of arbitrary justice administered by one man, Prime Min Hun Sen (M)

1999-01-03.title: Lusty New Papers Take On the Powerful in Mexico
1999-01-03.abstract: Alejandro Junco de la Vega is shaking Mexico's power elite through the newspapers he owns in country's three largest cities, Reforma in Mexico City, Mural in Guadalajara and El Norte in Monterrey; has fought freedom-of-information battles with top industrialists, Supreme Court, political parties, even Pres Ernesto Zedillo and his predecessors; acknowledges that key to survival is selling newspapers and advertising; growth of his media empire causes some concern that it may smother rivals that have also pioneered quality papers in regional cities; photo; map (M)

1999-01-03.title: Looking Past Hussein, U.S. Is Peering at a Pretender
1999-01-03.abstract: US officials meet with Sharif Ali bin al-Hussein, 42-year-old heir to throne of short-lived modern Iraqi monarchy, which ended in 1958; he lives in London, commands only a small movement and believes that a constitutional monarchy still has resonance in Iraq; photo (M)

1999-01-03.title: THE NEW EUROPE: United and Divided; Shiny, Prosperous 'Euroland' Has Some Cracks in Facade
1999-01-03.abstract: Europe has two faces, light and dark, and they are evident on Germany's western and eastern borders as the continent crowns four decades of integration with the adoption of a single currency, the euro.    Here in Aachen, which lay in ruins in 1945, borders have ceased to exist. It has become usual for people who work in this western German city to live in Belgium and play a weekly tennis match in the Netherlands. The sweet whiff of prosperity seeps from Italian shoe stores, Spanish tapas bars, French croissanteries and English pottery shops that line the pedestrian shopping areas on all sides of the borders.

1999-01-04.title: A Locked Door Is Broken, and Seoul Lawmakers Have a Spy Furor
1999-01-04.abstract: Ferocious political debate rages in South Korea over whether country's spies are running out of control; follows accusations by opposition member of National Assembly that national spy agency is using room in Assembly building as base to conduct surveillance on opposition members (M)

1999-01-04.title: DEAD ZONES: Fighting Back in India.; Calcutta's Prostitutes Lead the Fight on AIDS
1999-01-04.abstract: Prostitutes in Calcutta, India, have become leading crusaders in city's fight against AIDS, traveling like missionaries from brothel to brothel in Sonagachi red-light district to preach to madams, pimps and other prostitutes about importance of using condoms, most effective shield against HIV infection; has organized more than half of prostitutes in red-light enclave into force with influence to challenge any pimp or madam who would insist on customer's right to sex without condom, operating as kind of labor union for world's oldest profession; Indian Govt will use Sonagachi project as model in large-scale battle against AIDS, hoping that by acting swiftly while overall infection rate is relatively low it can avoid horrific burden of death that afflicts Africa; India has four million people estimated to be infected with virus; photos; map; chart (L)

1999-01-04.title: Cambodia Town's 'Luck' Leaves Illness in Its Wake
1999-01-04.abstract: 3,000 tons of industrial waste from Taiwan, dumped in field near Sihanoukville, Cambodia, has caused illness among poor villagers who regarded it as 'windfall' and used it to build tents, canopies and bedding and to store food; tests have determined that waste was contaminated with high levels of mercury and possibly hazardous mixture of other metals; Formosa Plastics Corp says it was unable to dump waste in Taiwan because of fear of public protests; photos (M)

1999-01-04.title: 2 Top Khmer Rouge Defectors End Tour
1999-01-04.abstract: Former Khmer Rouge leaders, Khieu Samphan and Nuon Chea, reportedly end six-day grand national reconciliation tour and return to Pailin, remote jungle town that has become well-guarded haven for defectors from Communist movement (S)

1999-01-04.title: Mexico City Journal; Tortilla Rises: Must Belts Tighten?
1999-01-04.abstract: Decision by Mexican Govt to end decades-old corn subsidies and price controls on tortillas, staple central to Mexican diet, causes concern and anger among millions of poor Mexicans who can ill afford anticipated 5-cents-per-pound increase in prices; officials say decision reflects effort by Pres Ernesto Zedillo to reduce Govt spending; say subsidies cost Govt $1 billion in 1998; photo (M)

1999-01-05.title: Gunmen Wound 2 Jewish Settlers in Hebron
1999-01-05.abstract: Gunmen open fire on van transporting Jewish settlers in Hebron, wounding two Israeli women; Israeli Army officials suspect that gunmen are Palestinian militants; photo (M)

1999-01-05.title: World Briefing
1999-01-05.abstract: THE AMERICAS    PERU: NEW PREMIER APPOINTED IN SHUFFLE -- With his support declining in polls, President Alberto Fujimori announced that he would shuffle his Cabinet, but not change the Government's economic policies. Victor Joy Way Rojas, the leader of the Congress and a longtime ally of Mr. Fujimori, will replace Alberto Pandolfi Arbulu as Prime Minister.    Clifford Krauss (NYT)

1999-01-05.title: 3d Blair Official Steps Down in Scandal Over Secret Loan
1999-01-05.abstract: Charlie Whelan resigns as press secretary to Chancellor of Exchequer Gordon Brown in scandal that has already cost Prime Min Tony Blair his closest political confidant, Trade and Industry Min Peter Mandelson, and Paymaster Gen Geoffrey Robinson; behind all three departures is secret $625,000 home loan from Robinson to Mandelson in 1996, which Mandelson failed to reveal even when Robinson's outside financial affairs became subject of investigation (M)

1999-01-05.title: U.S. READY TO EASE SOME RESTRICTIONS IN POLICY ON CUBA
1999-01-05.abstract: Senior American officials say US is ready to let more people and more money flow to Cuba, but they reject fundamental review of US policy toward Cuba; moves will let Cuban citizens receive millions more dollars from American friends and family members, allow them to buy more American food and medicine, permit more direct flights between two countries, renew direct mail service from US and let Baltimore Orioles and Cuban national baseball team play ball in March; Sec of State Madeleine K Albright formally recommends steps to Pres Clinton, and White House is expected to announce them on Jan 5; Administration officials say forthcoming report from Council on Foreign Relations helped to shape their new proposals (M)

1999-01-05.title: Haris Journal; Iranian Snapshot: Idle Townsmen, Hocus-Pocus
1999-01-05.abstract: Touring showmen and magicians are still fixture in depressed Iranian towns like Haris, where unemployed men have little else to entertain them; map; photo (M)

1999-01-06.title: 500 Are Massacred in Congo, Missionaries Report
1999-01-06.abstract: Missionary Service News Agency reports that about 500 people were massacred in eastern Congo during New Year holiday; if report is true, it would be largest massacre since start of rebellion in Aug against Congo Pres Laurent Kabila; Roman Catholic news agency says killings were carried out by soldiers aligned with rebels, who are led by Tutsi; map (M)

1999-01-06.title: Anti-Castro Exiles Won Limit on Changes
1999-01-06.abstract: Pres Clinton and Vice Pres Al Gore rejected idea of taking any large steps--or even opening policy toward Cuba to serious review--because of political power of anti-Castro Cuban-American communities in Florida and New Jersey; foreign policy experts, Catholic archbishops and senior Senators tried hard to push US to change its policy toward Cuba significantly (M)

1999-01-06.title: Tokyo Aide Questions 'Bubble-Like' U.S. Economy : Dollar Takes a Pounding As Investors Flock to Yen
1999-01-06.abstract: HONG KONG—  The dollar tumbled Tuesday to a 27-month low against the yen after a leading Japanese financial official described the U.S. economy as "bubble-like."

1999-01-06.title: Iranians Assert Rogue Officers Slew Dissidents
1999-01-06.abstract: Iran's Intelligence Ministry says that rogue Iranian intelligence officers were responsible for killings late last year of five prominent critics of Government's conservative wing; Government's public acknowledgment could suggest that, under Pres Mohammad Khatami, Iran may be becoming kind of country in which embarrassing secrets can no longer be permanently harbored; all five of those who were slain had criticized hard-line clerics within Iran's Government who have been principal opponents of Khatami's moves for greater moderation and openness; ministry's statement does not identify or say how many agents have been arrested; dissidents who were killed are Dariush Foruhar and his wife, Parvaneh, Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh, Mohammad Mokhtari and Majid Sharif (M)

1999-01-06.title: IRAQI'S ANGRY CALL FOR REVOLT SPLITS THE ARAB NATIONS
1999-01-06.abstract: Bitter war of words between Iraq and its Arab neighbors has followed last month's US-led bombings, leaving Iraq more isolated than at any time since Persian Gulf war; Iraq accuses Arabs of failing to stand by its side; Egypt has responded with particular scorn, all but calling for ouster of Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein; Hussein on Jan 5 calls on Arabs to rise up against leaders who have not given him their full support; only handful of Arab governments explicitly condemned four days of US and British air strikes, which ended on Dec 19, and in weeks since, none have been willing to align themselves with Iraq; Hussein photo (M)

1999-01-07.title: Israel's Crowd-Pleasing General Throws His (Civilian) Hat In
1999-01-07.abstract: Former Israeli Army Chief of Staff Amnon Lipkin-Shahak announces his centrist candidacy for Prime Minister; says Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu 'is dangerous for Israel' (M)

1999-01-07.title: U.S. SPIED ON IRAQ UNDER U.N. COVER, OFFICIALS NOW SAY
1999-01-07.abstract: US officials say that American spies worked undercover on teams of United Nations arms inspectors ferreting out secret Iraqi weapons programs; say American intelligence agencies provided information and technology to UN Special Commission (Unscom); say US and other nations received information on Iraqi weapons programs from inspectors; disclosure that American spies had worked as inspectors comes after Boston Globe and Washington Post reported that such agents had used team as cover to spy on Pres Saddam Hussein; US officials say that former American UN inspector, Scott Ritter, was misinformed when he said US had taken over UN intelligence operation in Iraq; Unscom chmn Richard Butler photo (M)

1999-01-07.title: Edward Windsor, Prince, Will Take Sophie . . .
1999-01-07.abstract: Buckinham Palace announces that Prince Edward, 34, Queen Elizabeth's youngest child, will wed Sophie Rhys-Jones, 33, this year; photo (M)

1999-01-07.title: French Premier Assails U.S. Over Iraq Air Strikes
1999-01-07.abstract: French Prime Min Lionel Jospin criticizes US and British air strikes against Iraq, and says that France will soon be ready to propose ways for ending impasse with Saddam Hussein (M)

1999-01-07.title: Detour on Capitalist Road: Die-Hard Maoist Collective
1999-01-07.abstract: Nanjie, China, is dinosaur in age often marked by economic free-for-all; its farm, factory, and housing are all collectively owned and managed; take-home pay of residents does not exceed $30 a month, but each family receives needs of life free; Nanjie, billed as socialist success, is often cited as paragon by China's 'leftists'--dwindling, old-guard communist critics of China's rush from ideology and state planning; fame of village suggests just how anomalous it is today--and how far leftists have fallen as Pres Jiang Zemin leads country toward market economy; map; photo (M)

1999-01-08.title: WORLD BRIEFING
1999-01-08.abstract: Irish Republican Army says it is committed to Northern Ireland peace settlement but feels 'growing frustration' over delays over putting it into place (S)

1999-01-08.title: 'Amnesiac' Gets His Name, and a Jail Cell
1999-01-08.abstract: Young man who attracted international attention with his strange tale of waking up in a Montreal parking lot in October without any memory finally finds his identity when Montreal police show up to arrest him; police charge him with public mischief and obstruction of justice for deliberately misleading investigators about his identity; last fall, he told doctors he thought his name was James Edward Brighton, and he believed he was from New Jersey; police say he is actually Matthew Honeycuttt, 28, from Tennessee, where he is being investigated for fraud (S)

1999-01-08.title: Reports of Spying Dim Outlook for Inspections
1999-01-08.abstract: Reports that United States used the United Nations weapons inspectors in Iraq as cover for spying on Saddam Hussein are dimming any chances that inspection system will survive and that its head, Richard Butler, will remain in his job much longer (M)

1999-01-08.title: Colombian Rebel Chief Is Absent as Peace Talks Start
1999-01-08.abstract: Colombia's first steps toward talks aimed at ending its civil war stumble as commander of largest rebel group does not appear at an opening ceremony with Pres Andres Pastrana; commander, Manuel Marulanda, has not appeared in public since his movement began in 1964; rebel leaders in attendance say he stayed away because of death threats; photo of Pastrana (M)

1999-01-08.title: U.S. Jet Fires Missile at an Iraqi Battery After Being Targeted
1999-01-08.abstract: Iraqi antiaircraft battery, pressing its challenge against American and British control of its skies, turns its radar on American fighter jet, which responds by firing missile at it; map shows no-flight zones in Iraq (M)

1999-01-09.title: Beijing Journal; China's Pigeons Perch on Pedestal, Not Just Statues
1999-01-09.abstract: Outpouring of support for Beijing's pigeon population follows unsolved poisoning of 118 birds in public plaza; photo (Beijing Journal) (M)

1999-01-09.title: UNHEEDED WARNINGS: A special report.; Before Bombings, Omens and Fears
1999-01-09.abstract: Special report, Unheeded Warnings, on failure of State Department to act on information pointing to threats to United States embassies in months preceding Aug 7, 1998, attacks in Tanzania and Kenya; Prudence Bushnell, Ambassador to Kenya, had been repeatedly turned down in her requests for a more secure embassy in face of terrorist threats, and other warnings came from Central Intelligence Agency, Federal Bureau of Investigation, and Gen Anhony C Zinni, commander of US Central Command; new report made public by commission appointed by Sec Madeleine Albright excoriates State Department for failing to safeguard US missions, particularly for giving vulnerable missions like one in Nairobi low priority; maps; photos (M)

1999-01-09.title: Iraq Damage More Severe Than Reported, Pentagon Says
1999-01-09.abstract: Chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, Gen Henry H Shelton, and commander of US forces in Persian Gulf, Gen Anthony C Zinni, say that US and British raids on Iraq that stopped three weeks ago caused more damage than initially reported (M)

1999-01-09.title: Malaysian Prime Minister Acts to Consolidate Power
1999-01-09.abstract: KUALA LUMPUR—  CORRECTION PUBLISHED IN THE INTERNATIONAL HERALD TRIBUNE ON JANUARY 12, 1998:

1999-01-09.title: Report on Security Suggests Closing Some U.S. Embassies
1999-01-09.abstract: Special panels set up by State Department to investigate bombings of two US embassies in Africa recommend closing a number of embassies around world that are vulnerable to terrorist attack and replacing them with regional 'superembassies'; also recommends that department seek extra $1.4 billion a year to upgrade security at scores of diplomatic posts; photo (M)

1999-01-10.title: In a Parish in Peru: The Priest, His 2 Sons and a Very Angry Bishop
1999-01-10.abstract: Roman Catholic Church hierarchy threatens to suspend all charitable donations to tiny town of Morrope, Peru, because its Catholic priest, Rev Jorge Arbanil Rico, has two teenage children whom he fathered years after he entered priesthood; local Bishop has forbidden him from baptizing or marrying; Arbanil Rico is unrepentant; against orders of church hierarchy, he offers Mass in town cemetery and in streets; this is first time in modern Peruvian history that priest has defied Roman Catholic hierarchy by continuing to practice priestly functions while openly admitting to having broken vow of celibacy; Arbanil Rico's case is not unique; there are other Peruvian priests with children, but these other would-be rebels received quiet acquiescence of their local bishops; photo; map (M)

1999-01-10.title: Ntsu Mokhehle, 80; Fractious Land's Premier
1999-01-10.abstract: Ntsu Mokhele, longtime leader of Lesotho, dies after falling ill at his 80th birthday party in December; photo (S)

1999-01-10.title: Moving Gingerly Toward Civilian Rule, Nigerians Cast Ballots
1999-01-10.abstract: Nigerians elect governors and state legislators; voting is second step in military government's plan to restore civilian rule by May; country's borders are sealed during voting hours to prevent vote-rigging; vote, in 35 of Nigeria's 36 states, should give foreign monitors further evidence of how fair presidential elections next month will be; results should also provide clues to whether emerging alliance between two of three parties can check seemingly inevitable rise of Gen Olusegun Obasanjo to presidency (M)

1999-01-10.title: A Canadian Rousts Diplomacy (and Ruffles the U.S.)
1999-01-10.abstract: Canada's Foreign Minister, Lloyd Axworthy, has made waves during his three years in office, often by running up against United States; Axworthy bristles at suggestion that he or his initiatives are anti-American, but he refuses to consider United States 'the indispensable nation' that Secretary of State Madeleine K Albright has declared it to be; Axworthy feels that even though US is world's only superpower, countries like Canada can set the pace; photo (M)

1999-01-10.title: British Researchers on Animal Rights Death List
1999-01-10.abstract: Animal rights extremists in Britain have threatened to kill 10 scientists who conduct medical testing on animals; Scotland Yard's special antiterror branch has separate data bank devoted to these fringe groups; they have safe houses, secret bank accounts, codes, Internet mailing lists and bomb-making instruction manuals; Prof Colin Blakemore, physiologist who has won awards for advances in treating vision problems, is on list of threatened scientists; he argues it is impossible to come up with any form of conventional medical treatment that has not at some point depended on research on animals; mail bomb was sent to his home; photo (M)

1999-01-11.title: Officer Is Denied Amnesty In the Killing of Steve Biko
1999-01-11.abstract: South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission denies amnesty to former police officer George Bizos, implicated in murder of anti-apartheid activist Steve Biko in 1977; reportedly ruled that amnesty could not be granted since Bizos has not admitted committing crime (M)

1999-01-11.title: Observers Give Voting In Nigeria Good Marks
1999-01-11.abstract: Officials monitoring Nigeria's countrywide elections say they had been generally peaceful and well organized; say they are improvement over local elections in December 1998 and another positive step toward presidential contest in February 1999; preliminary results in races for governors and legislators in 35 of 36 states show country's three political parties scored victories in ethnic and geographic bases; map; photo (M)

1999-01-11.title: 'If We Are Going to Bail Out the Company, Why Does He Have to Stay?' : Malaysia Turns Its Back on Tycoons
1999-01-11.abstract: KUALA LUMPUR—  When critics complain about Malaysia's brand of crony capitalism, the name that usually comes up first is Halim Saad, a boyish-looking millionaire who runs the country's largest infrastructure company.

1999-01-11.title: French Right Bickers After a Political Deal With Left in Lyons
1999-01-11.abstract: Leaders of French conservative political parties that lost last national elections in 1997 are bickering over victory by Anne-Marie Comparini, member of Union for French Democracy, in election to head Rhone-Alpes regional parliament; Comparini won regional post after 37-hour marathon of 'shady bartering' described by party colleague as 'disgusting spectacle;' her victory, mostly with leftist support, plunges right yet further into disarray; is blow to Charles Millon, former Defense Minister, who had been head of regional parliament since 1988 and who had been losing ground since spring 1998, when he accepted support from extreme-right national front (M)

1999-01-11.title: Vatican Aide In Canada Cited In Threat Case
1999-01-11.abstract: Vatican's elegant diplomatic compound in Ottawa, usually one of most tranquil and circumspect embassies in Canada, has been focus of much attention following charges by local police that Msgr Vito Rallo, second-highest representative of Pope in Canada, harassed former employee Josephine Greco and threatened to kill her; attorney for Rallo says charges are without merit (M)

1999-01-12.title: Johannesburg Journal; A Post-Apartheid Tribute: Art Leading the People
1999-01-12.abstract: Interview with Reshada Crouse, South African artist who is completing mural in Johannesburg's Civic Center depicting role played by artists and theater people in anti-apartheid struggle; photo (M)

1999-01-12.title: World Briefing
1999-01-12.abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: COMPROMISE IS PROPOSED -- Britain's Northern Ireland Secretary proposed a compromise on disarmament of the Irish Republican Army. The Secretary, Mo Mowlam, agreed in effect with the position held by Sinn Fein, the political wing of the I.R.A., that the Belfast peace agreement does not require the I.R.A. to disarm before May 2000. But she said she hoped that the I.R.A. would make some gesture toward disarmament sooner than that.    James F. Clarity(NYT)

1999-01-12.title: Europe Jetmaker Claims Half the Market : Airbus in Ascendancy
1999-01-12.abstract: PARIS—  Airbus Industrie said Monday it had achieved its goal of winning half of the world market for big commercial jets, and company executives said the European consortium was in a position to compete on an equal basis with Boeing Co. of the United States.

1999-01-12.title: A Killer's Ignorance Adds Poignancy to Biko Case
1999-01-12.abstract: Full text of decision by South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission denying amnesty for former police officer Gideon Nieuwoudt, charged in death of anti-apartheid activist Steve Biko in 1977; indicates Nieuwoudt did not know how important Biko really was and is therefore unable to claim political motivation or to insist crime was committed to suppress anti-apartheid movement--major qualification for amnesty (S)

1999-01-12.title: Sturdy Pillar of Turkey's Left Names Cabinet
1999-01-12.abstract: Turkey's six-week-old political crisis may have ended with announcement by former Prime Min Bulent Ecevit, secular politician, of formation of minority government that is expected to win parliamentary approval; Govt will be sixth since last election in December 1995, which produced Govt headed by Islamic leader Necmettin Erbakan; country has been without functioning Government since November 1998, when Prime Min Mesut Yilmaz was implicated in corruption scandal and lost confidence vote in Parliament; Ecevit photo (M)

1999-01-13.title: World Briefing
1999-01-13.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: LAW LORDS TO RULE ON PINOCHET -- Seven Law Lords, instead of the usual five, will decide whether Gen. Augusto Pinochet, as former Chilean head of state,  enjoys immunity from arrest or must face extradition proceedings to put him on trial in Spain for crimes against humanity. The hearing, which starts Jan. 18, was prompted by the unprecedented setting aside last month of an earlier judgment against the former dictator because one judge failed to disclose his wife's involvement with Amnesty International, a party to the case.   Warren Hoge (NYT)    RUSSIA: A NEW FIGHTER -- A MIG fighter jet was unveiled outside Moscow, though the plane has yet to carry out its maiden flight. The ''multifunctional fighter'' has been kept under wraps since development began in the late 1980's. The aircraft has only carried out runs on the airfield but is expected to take to the air at the end of February, said its chief designer, Mikhail Korzhuyev.   (AP)

1999-01-13.title: Saudi Arabia Is Denounced By Baghdad : Saddam Turns Up Heat in Mideast
1999-01-13.abstract: PARIS—  The battle to sway Arab public opinion about Iraq broadened Tuesday as Baghdad accused Saudi Arabia’s rulers of betraying the Arab cause and colluding with the United States to punish the Iraqi people — the first time that the government of Saddam Hussein has lashed out so pointedly against the leading conservative Arab government in the Gulf.

1999-01-13.title: Iraq Is Said to Shun Vital Food and Medicine It Could Import Under U.N. Sanctions
1999-01-13.abstract: Diplomats and United Nations officials say that evidence is growing that Pres Saddam Hussein has put off buying or distributing vital food and medicine that he can import through limited oil sales; they speak as pressure mounts to lift economic sanctions on Iraq to improve lives of its people; officials say childhood nutrition is of particular concern; nearly $25 million has been budgeted for high-protein biscuits and fortified milk for children under 5 and nursing mothers since last fall, but Iraqis have not placed any orders for goods despite repeated urging for several months by United Nations officials, including Sec Gen Kofi Annan (M)

1999-01-13.title: Function vs. Fantasy :Prada and Gucci Slug It Out
1999-01-13.abstract: THEY are the fashion titans of the 1990s. In one corner, a streak of blood red across the sleek hair, scarlet sequins dripping on the jacket and the crescendo from ‘‘Psycho’’ on the soundtrack.

1999-01-13.title: Hague Journal; Then It Was the Klan. Now It's the Balkan Agony.
1999-01-13.abstract: Profile of Gabrielle Kirk McDonald, 56, who is only American among 14 judges of International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia; year ago she was elected its president; she deals with trials that address some of many war crimes that were committed during breakup of Yugoslavia; as young black woman, McDonald's early professional years were spent as civil rights lawyer for NAACP Legal Defense Fund; photo (M)

1999-01-14.title: Venice Journal; Boat Gobbles Waves, but Bobbles in Political Seas
1999-01-14.abstract: Charles W Robinson, wealthy American who owns palazzo in Venice, has developed wave-eating boat which he says could ease erosion along city's age-old canals; he must now contend with civic indecision that seems inborn in that city; photo (M)

1999-01-14.title: European Parliament's Censure Motion on EU Commission Collapses
1999-01-14.abstract: PARIS—  Correction published in the Saturday, January 16, 1999 issue of the International Herald Tribune :

1999-01-14.title: France, in Break With U.S., Urges End to Iraq Embargo
1999-01-14.abstract: France makes formal break with United States and Britain, proposing that United Nations Security Council lift oil embargo on Iraq and institute new weapons monitoring system to prevent Saddam Hussein from rearming; US rejects most major points in French proposal in advance; initial reaction from Iraq, which would have to agree to new monitoring system, is largely negative; French emphasize that their proposals are meant to generate debate and some action in Security Council, which has been largely inert on subject of Iraq since American and British bombing raids last month (M)

1999-01-14.title: Secret Tape of Plotter's Confession Deepens the Mystery of a Political Murder in Mexico
1999-01-14.abstract: Compelling new piece of evidence implicates Raul Salinas, brother of former Mexican Pres Carlos Salinas de Gortari, in 1994 assassination of Jose Francisco Ruiz Massieu, ruling party's No 2 official; sensational trial of Raul Salinas has taken so many bizarre turns that it has done little to dispel notion that Mexico's high and mighty enjoy impunity; new evidence is tape recording of very first interrogation of Jorge Rodriguez Gonzalez, who organized killing and is serving 37-year jail sentence as accomplice to murder; Rodriguez Gonzalez affirms repeatedly that it was Raul Salinas who gave orders to 'eliminate' Ruiz Massieu, fast-rising politician; he also says that Raul Salinas Lozano, father of Raul and Carlos, also approved killing of Ruiz Massieu, who was his former son-in-law; chart showing principals in case; photos (L)

1999-01-14.title: Gore Says U.S. Is Willing to Help Iraq Get More Food and Medicine
1999-01-14.abstract: Vice Pres Al Gore says White House is willing to consider ways to loosen restrictions on Iraq's ability to buy food and medicine; emphasizes that Clinton Administration's differences are with Saddam Hussein, not with Iraqi people (M)

1999-01-15.title: Haitian Leader's Foes Raise The Specter of Dictatorship
1999-01-15.abstract: Haitian opposition leaders accuse Pres Rene Preval of trying to establish virtual dictatorship, although there is little public outcry so far over Preval's decision to bypass parliament and form government by decree; political foes suspect maneuvers by Preval and his mentor, former Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide (S)

1999-01-15.title: Rights Report on Mexico Says Abuses Continue
1999-01-15.abstract: Human Rights Watch reports that police and soldiers commonly kidnap, torture and kill people in Mexico, despite Government claims that such abuses have ended (S)

1999-01-15.title: Study of Prehumans' Teeth Suggests That They Dined on Meat
1999-01-15.abstract: Anthropologists report that prehuman ancestors who lived in Africa three million years ago dined not only on fruits and leaves, as chimpanzees do, but may have already become meat eaters; tests of carbon content of teeth of Australopithecus africanus indicate they ate large quantities of food rich in carbon 13, grasses or sedges, or animals that ate such plants, or both; species climbed trees but also walked upright, and ventured out of forest to forage in open grassland; Matt Sponheimer of Rutgers explains tooth analysis he did with Dr Julia A Lee-Thorp, South African paleoanthropologist (M)

1999-01-15.title: Channel Journal; The Joy of Duty-Free Shopping, Soon to Turn Sour
1999-01-15.abstract: Channel ferries carrying passengers between Britain and the Continent have become floating duty-free shopping centers in recent years, but sprees will end in June by decree of European Commission, unless British, French and German governments are able to reverse decision; eager British shoppers comment; photos (Channel Journal) (S)

1999-01-15.title: EU Commission Survives, But Seems to Lose Power
1999-01-15.abstract: STRASBOURG—  The European Parliament failed Thursday in an attempt to oust the European Commission over allegations of graft and incompetence, but many of its members said the vote marked a significant shift of power toward the 626-seat elected assembly.

1999-01-16.title: Lawyer for Raul Salinas Disputes The Validity of New Evidence
1999-01-16.abstract: Eduardo Luengo Creel, chief lawyer representing Raul Salinas de Gortari, who is on trial in Mexico for 1994 assassination of Jose Francisco Ruiz Massieu, questions validity of newly uncovered piece of evidence that prosecutors say adds weight to homicide accusations against client; says tape recordings of Jorge Rodriguez Gonzalez, key witness in case, is not new evidence and does not prove anything (S)

1999-01-16.title: Thousands of Farmers Protest in China; 1 Dies in Police Clash
1999-01-16.abstract: Thousands of farmers in Daolin Township in Hunan Province, China, clash with hundreds of police officers in latest and perhaps largest in series of farmer and worker protests that have sprung up nationwide in past year; were reportedly protesting against excessive and arbitrary taxes; clash reportedly leaves one person dead and dozens wounded (M)

1999-01-16.title: Russia Proposes New System For Monitoring Iraqi Arms
1999-01-16.abstract: Russia, which opposes any revival of present arms inspection in Iraq, gives UN Security Council detailed blueprint for new monitoring apparatus having much less independence; plan is quickly rejected by US, which seeks to retain present system and would allow limited sales of Iraqi oil provided revenues went solely to buy civilian goods to alleviate strains of eight years of sanctions (M)

1999-01-16.title: Sudanese Foes Agree to Extend Cease-Fire in Famine Region
1999-01-16.abstract: Main warring factions in Sudan agree to extend cease-fire in part of country hardest hit by famine (M)

1999-01-16.title: Where Oil Is Plentiful But Cash Is Short
1999-01-16.abstract: Persian Gulf monarchies that depend on oil for three-quarter of incomes have seen revenues decline by 30 percent, or $25 billion, over period of year; reversal has led even politicians to recommend adjustments that would end old ways of showering citizens with benficence while keeping outsiders at arm's length; for quarter-century, oil has allowed gulf states self-sufficiency and political leverage far in excess of what comparably sized countries could hope to achieve, and has formed dependable pillar of legitimacy for region's six monarchies; photo; graph (M)

1999-01-17.title: U.S. Weighs Its Reaction To Massacre In Kosovo
1999-01-17.abstract: Clinton Administration struggles to explain how it will react to execution-style massacre of 45 ethnic Albanians by Serbian forces under control of Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic; Pres Clinton condemns killings and calls them 'clear violation' of Serbs' cease-fire agreement with NATO; senior official says Administration is stopping short of calling Serbs in 'substantial noncompliance' with NATO agreement (M)

1999-01-17.title: Brazilian, in I.M.F. Talks, May Ask for Early Aid
1999-01-17.abstract: Brazil Finance Min Pedro Malan begins talks with International Monetary Fund in Washington, DC; says he might ask for early release of next round of international assistance to country to help stabilize its economy; talks begin day after Brazil dropped efforts to defend value of its currency, raising fears of another bout of global financial turmoil and economic hardship (M)

1999-01-17.title: Pentagon Presses Ex-Iraq Inspector on Book
1999-01-17.abstract: Defense Dept demands that Scott Ritter, former United Nations weapons inspector in Iraq, turn over advance copies of book in which he is expected to step up his criticism of Clinton Administration and detail some of his work for UN; photo (M)

1999-01-17.title: North Korea And U.S. Meet On Inspection Of Atom Plant
1999-01-17.abstract: US and North Korea open new talks in Geneva on whether North Korea will allow inspections of suspected underground nuclear facility; talks precede new round of four-way meetings aimed at reaching formal peace treaty for peninsula, where war ended 46 years ago; this will be fourth such gathering in past 13 months of North and South Korea and their chief allies, China and US; map (M)

1999-01-17.title: Still Locked in Storm's Horror, Hondurans Are Fearful of Future
1999-01-17.abstract: Article on how Santa Rosa de Aguan, Honduras, is trying to rebuild after Hurricane Mitch; notes that little has improved in two months after hurricane struck and discusses plight of some village residents; map; photos (M)

1999-01-18.title: Turkey's New Leader Vows April Elections, but Delay Is Possible
1999-01-18.abstract: Bulent Ecevit takes office as Turkey's new Prime Min after easily winning confidence vote in Parliament; pledges to lead country smoothly into elections on April 18; military may press Parliament to postpone elections as public opinion polls suggest Islamic-oriented Virtue Party remains country's strongest; commanders wish to prevent party from gaining new power (M)

1999-01-18.title: Sinn Fein Negotiator Predicts Success of Peace Talks for Ulster
1999-01-18.abstract: Martin McGuinness, chief negotiator for Sinn Fein, political wing of Irish Republican Army, says growing crisis in Northern Ireland peace effort will be resolved by end of March, target date for British Govt to return home rule powers to northern province of Ulster (M)

1999-01-18.title: Storm Victims Surge North, With U.S. as Goal
1999-01-18.abstract: US officials report alarming increase in number of immigrants trying to enter US through Mexico, ever since Hurricane Mitch devastated Honduras and Guatemala leaving thousands dead and millions homeless; say latest group, driven by loss of lives and livelihoods, is more desperate and more deterimned to reach US; report significant increases in numbers of Central Americans detained at Mexican border in December; Mexican officials worry increased number crossing border of southern Mexico could indicate beginning of large and prolonged migration of Central Americans through Mexico to US; say such migration would place even greater burdens on border control systems in Mexico and US and put untold numbers of immigrants at risk; provided services to approximately 3,000 Central American immigrants in December, or 10 times number in December 1997; photos; map (M)

1999-01-18.title: Dead Dream: Brazil's Plan Is Blocked
1999-01-18.abstract: News analysis of decision by Brazil's Govt to stop supporting currency and to allow it to float freely; decision marks end of celebrated monetary plan that promised to transform Latin America's biggest country into modern nation; plan foundered because of Govt's inability to maneuver reform past vested interests that enjoy benefits and breaks Brazil cannot afford (M)

1999-01-18.title: U.S. and EU Denounce Massacre in Kosovo as NATO Ponders Next Move
1999-01-18.abstract: PARIS—  The United States and the European Union reacted with outrage to the massacre of 45 ethnic Albanians in the Yugoslav province of Kosovo, with President Bill Clinton saying that "this was a deliberate and indiscriminate act of murder designed to sow fear among the people of Kosovo."

1999-01-19.title: DEFIANT YUGOSLAV ORDERS EXPULSION OF  U.S.  DIPLOMAT
1999-01-19.abstract: Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic defies Western allies and threatens fragile cease-fire in Kosovo province by ordering expulsion of William Walker, US diplomat who blamed Serbs for massacre of more than 40 ethnic Albanians near village of Racak; move may force Clinton Administration to withdraw 700 unarmed monitors trying to enforce cease-fire; top NATO military officials Gen Wesley K Clark and Gen Klaus Neumann will meet with Milosevic in Belgrade; will urge him to back down from order to expel Walker and to take seriously recent NATO warnings to restrain forces and reduce numbers in province to agreed levels; Serbian forces refuse entry of Hague war crimes tribunal prosecutor Louise Arbour into Kosovo to investigate killings; again attack area surrounding Racak, village where massacre took place on January 15, despite warnings from NATO; photos (M)

1999-01-19.title: China Tightens Rein on Writers And Publishers
1999-01-19.abstract: China's powerful Department of Propaganda is engaged in quiet crackdown on liberal intellectuals, journalists and publications; has sent stern warnings to some magazines and newspapers that strayed too far from prescribed line, forced some editors and writers out of jobs and halted distribution of several books that delve into political alternatives or embarrassing episodes in history of Communist rule; campaign reflects leadership's concern for stability as it confronts rising unemployment and protests by workers and farmers; may also reflect concern over dissent related to upcoming 10th anniversary of violent pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square and 50th anniversary of founding of People's Republic (M)

1999-01-19.title: Russian Biologist Denies Work in Iran On Germ Weapons
1999-01-19.abstract: Dr Valery Bakayev, Russian biologist in Iran, denies conducting research that could have helped Soviet Union or Iran develop germ weapons; says while he worked at Pasteur Institute in Teheran for more than five years, work focused solely on civilian development of vaccines against diseases and on other nonmilitary research (S)

1999-01-19.title: U.N. Chief Faults Both Sides For Dashing Angola Hopes
1999-01-19.abstract: UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan, in report to Security Council, accuses Angolan Govt and Unita rebel organization of destroying all hopes of peace in Angola; says country is on verge of catastrophic breakdown, with malnutrition and disease rising as fighting spreads, leaving hundreds of thousands of civilians on run from towns and villages under attack; says with recent resumption of fighting, conditions for meaningful UN peacekeeping role has ceased to exist; proposed appointing New York-based special envoy for Angola; says, for moment, all that remains for agency to do is to safely remove 1,000 international monitors and aid workers and try to prevent loss of millions of dollars of equipment (M)

1999-01-19.title: Istanbul Journal; You Want a Black Olive Bagel? Come to Turkey
1999-01-19.abstract: Tribeca, first bagel cafe in Istanbul, Turkey, is among many signs of growing American influence in country where citizens seem to crave everything American; serves American-style bagels, coffee and pastry, and 80 percent of its patrons are Turks; proprietors Rafi Karako and Hikmet Ongan comment; photo (M)

1999-01-20.title: NATO Fails in Belgrade Talks to Halt Kosovo Attacks
1999-01-20.abstract: NATO Gens Wesley K Clark and Klaus Neumann meet with Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic, Belgrade, to demand he identify those responsible for massacre of ethnic Albanians in Racak, reduce Serbian forces in Kosovo to previously agreed levels, revoke order expelling American Amb William Walker and let war crimes prosecutor Louise Arbour enter Kosovo; win no concessions; Clinton Administration officials say they do not favor immediate air attacks on Serbia; fighting continues in Racak (M)

1999-01-20.title: NATO's Kosovo Bid Is Rebuffed by Serbs
1999-01-20.abstract: Two NATO generals who flew to Belgrade to face down President Slobodan Milosevic on the issue of violence in Kosovo, the largely ethnic Albanian province in southern Serbia, failed to win any of the concessions they were seeking, Clinton Administration officials said.    Despite Mr. Milosevic's continuing defiance of a cease-fire in Kosovo, Administration officials said they did not favor immediate air attacks on Serbia. Still, they said they are aware that Mr. Milosevic is putting NATO's credibility to the test.

1999-01-20.title: King Hussein Back Home; Despite Joy, Jordan Frets
1999-01-20.abstract: King Hussein returns to tumultous welcome in Jordan after six months of cancer treatment in United States; weighs naming one of his sons to replace his brother, Crown Prince Hassan, as his designated successor; photo (M)

1999-01-20.title: As Angola Erupts, Youths Fear Being Forced to Fight
1999-01-20.abstract: Angolan youths are being required to register for draft as civil war heats up; fear possible resumption of use of press gangs to round up recruits; photo (M)

1999-01-20.title: Tense Times on Front Line of Brazil's Battle on Hyperinflation: Empty Shops
1999-01-20.abstract: Scarcity of shoppers is hopeful sign that Brazil may be able to avert return to hyperinflation after abandoning effort to peg currency to dollar; prices of imported goods are rising; photo (M)

1999-01-21.title: A Tale of a Cambodian Woman: Assigning the Guilt for Genocide
1999-01-21.abstract: Possible trial of Khmer Rouge leaders raises largely ignored question of how to deal with many thousands of functionaries who carried out brutal policies that led to deaths of over million Cambodians from 1975 to 1979; Neang Kin, now subsistence farmer, served 18 months in prison after confessing in 1979 to role in prison tortures, which she now denies; resents apparent impunity of leaders but fears their trials would again make her target; map; photos (M)

1999-01-21.title: U.S.I.A. Says Hacker Killed Web Site and Damaged Computer
1999-01-21.abstract: United States Information Agency says an outsider breached its Internet security, knocking out its Web site and forcing it to re-evaluate its computer protection; sabotage is second such incident in six months (M)

1999-01-21.title: Holbrooke Accused Of Ethics Violation
1999-01-21.abstract: The Justice Department has accused Richard C. Holbrooke, President Clinton's choice to be the top American diplomat at the United Nations, of a civil violation of ethics laws involving business contacts three years ago with the United States Embassy in South Korea. The investigation began as a result of allegations about improper lobbying in Hungary in 1995, but officials said yesterday that no evidence of wrongdoing was found in that case.   Article, page A3.

1999-01-21.title: World Briefing
1999-01-21.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: PEERS' VOTING RIGHTS -- Prime Minister Tony Blair's Government submitted to Parliament legislation aimed at fulfilling his promise to eliminate the voting rights of 750 hereditary peers, members of the House of Lords who gained their seats by the circumstances of birth. Some 500 life peers would remain in a transitional assembly while a proposed Royal Commission produced recommendations due Dec. 31 for longer-term reform of the chamber.    Warren Hoge (NYT)

1999-01-21.title: E-Mail to U.S. Lands Chinese Internet Entrepreneur in Jail
1999-01-21.abstract: Shanghai court sentences Lin Hai to two years in jail for giving 30,000 Chinese E-mail addresses to VIP Reference, electronic publication compiled by Chinese democracy advocates in Washington; case is closely watched by people monitoring Chinese efforts to control growing Internet use; photos (M)

1999-01-22.title: Warily, Cambodian Backs U.N. Plans for Khmer Rouge Trials
1999-01-22.abstract: Cambodian Prime Min Hun Sen confirms to UN that he supports setting up tribunal to try Khmer Rouge leaders; warns that any trial must not threaten Cambodia's newly achieved peace and reconciliation efforts (S)

1999-01-22.title: Missile Plan Puts U.S. in Quandary on China
1999-01-22.abstract: Clinton Administration, having planned major outlay for defense missiles, now faces major diplomatic and military quandary with China; anxieties over North Korea's missile firing over Japan last August underlie US plan to develop limited national system of missile defense, at proposed cost of $10.5 billion over six years; Sec William Cohen recently discussed joint venture with Japanese officials, raising Chinese fears about Japan's military ambitions; US troops in South Korea and Japan would be vulnerable to improved North Korean missiles; analyst Richard Armitage notes irony that China's client, North Korea, brought debate into daylight (S)

1999-01-22.title: Charting a Massacre: The Monitors' Report
1999-01-22.abstract: Excerpts from international monitors' chronological report on massacre of ethnic Albanians in Racak, Kosovo, by Serbian police (S)

1999-01-22.title: Security Council Seeks Talks With Angola Over U.N. Mission
1999-01-22.abstract: UN Security Council calls for talks with Angolan Government on future of its beleaguered mission there; Government recently resume fighting with entrenched rebel movement, and apparently wants UN to get out of way (S)

1999-01-22.title: Pakistan Denies Role in Plotting Bombings in India
1999-01-22.abstract: Ashraf Qazi, Pakistani envoy in New Delhi, denies that Bangladeshi arrested and accused of plotting to bomb American consulates in Madras and Calcutta is Pakistani intelligence agent, as police charge; notes that US and Pakistan have good relationship; US counterrorism team will interview suspect, Sayed Abu Nasir (S)

1999-01-23.title: 45 Die and 100 Are Hurt as Indonesian Muslims and Christians Riot
1999-01-23.abstract: Four days of rioting between Muslims and Christians on Ambon island causes deaths of 45 people in worst violence in Indonesia since May; police say more than 100 people are injured, 88 houses destroyed and 3 mosques and 3 churches burned; night curfew is imposed, and 2,500 soldiers and police officers work to restore order; riots were set off by street argument; map (M)

1999-01-23.title: Canada, at U.N., Proposes a 'Third Way' to Deal With the Iraqis
1999-01-23.abstract: Canada hopes to fill void between hardened Russian and American policies toward Iraq; Security Council begins discussing 'third way' proposed by Canada; Canada, with support of Brazil, this month's Council president, is calling for comprehensive assessments on status of Iraqi disarmament and on humanitarian situation in Iraq (M)

1999-01-23.title: Force Possible If Milosevic Stays Defiant, West Warns
1999-01-23.abstract: Western powers warn Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic that political solution must be found for Kosovo or he risks threat of NATO air strikes; say simply allowing head of international monitoring mission to remain there is not enough; NATO's supreme Commander, Gen Wesley K Clark, underlines threat of force; photo; map (M)

1999-01-23.title: Hong Kong Blames Foreign Managers for Airport's Faulty Start
1999-01-23.abstract: Government commission in Hong Kong lays most of blame for problems at new $20 billion airport to its Western managers; says managers' overconfidence prevented them from seeking delay in opening to fix bugs in airport's flight information screens, baggage-handling systems and vast cargo terminal; commission exonerates Government; all glitches have been worked out (M)

1999-01-23.title: Pope, in Mexico, Urges Moral Pressure on U.S. by Bishops
1999-01-23.abstract: Pope John Paul II arrives in Mexico City and is welcomed by thousands of followers; will meet in Mexico with bishops from all the Americas to underscore his point that churches should work more closely; also wants his bishops to pool their strengths to exert greater moral pressure on Washington; in Mexico, as everywhere else, Pope intends to remind people of dangers of materialism; photo (M)

1999-01-24.title: Nazi Funds Estimate Put at $750 Million
1999-01-24.abstract: Search by independent auditors for unclaimed deposits placed in Switzerland during World War II is expected to conclude by end of March 1999 and to turn up funds that would be worth $750 million with interest (S)

1999-01-24.title: Pope Urges Bishops to Minister to the Rich
1999-01-24.abstract: Pope John Paul II delivers Apostolic Exhortation to Bishops of North and South America during visit to Mexico City, urging them to take better care of poor while also instructing them to minister to rich; issues warning against increasing secularization of society; lashes out at inequities of unbridled free-market system; photo (M)

1999-01-24.title: Gingerly, Russia's Premier Tries to Fill Vacuum at Top
1999-01-24.abstract: Russian Prime Min Yevgeny M Primakov is trying to fill vacuum left by ailing Pres Boris Yeltsin by assuming more and more of Yeltsin's duties, but must be careful not to show any unseemly enthusiasm for job; public opinion polls indicate Primakov has been filling gap well enough to make himself Russia's most trusted politician; he has earned points simply for restoring impression of political stability (M)

1999-01-24.title: Social Insecurity in Brazil For Its Government Retirees
1999-01-24.abstract: They have been called ''privilegiados'' who collect lavish pensions and benefits, and portrayed as budgetary vampires who drain the pension system and contribute nothing.    Now, millions of retired Government workers across Brazil are watching the financial crisis that is enveloping Latin America's largest economy tear the first breaches in the social contract that has shaped their lives. What else, they wonder, will be open to question in the new scheme of things?

1999-01-24.title: South African Assassinated, Raising Fears For Election
1999-01-24.abstract: A high-level official of a South African multiracial political party, the United Democratic Movement, was assassinated today, riddled with bullets as he left a grocery store near his home in a rural area about an hour's drive north of Durban.     With the campaign for South Africa's second post-apartheid elections just gearing up, the killing of the official, Sifiso Nkabinde, once again raises questions about whether political violence will return as the vote approaches.

1999-01-25.title: Million Mexicans Bond With Pope at Mass
1999-01-25.abstract: Pope John Paul II celebrates outdoor mass in Spanish for over million people, Mexico City; includes special mention of Mexico's indigenous peoples in homily; photo (M)

1999-01-25.title: 47 Suspected Militants in India Charged in Missionary's Death
1999-01-25.abstract: Indian police charge 47 members of militant Hindu youth group with setting fire that killed Australian missionary Graham Staines and his sons Philip, 10, and Timothy, 6, as they slept inside van in remote tribal village of Orissa state; escalation in attacks on Christians spurs round of official condemnations; Prime Min Atal Behari Bajpayee says group, linked to his ruling party, will be punished if it was behind attack; many of attacks have occurred in tribal areas, where militants say Christians forcibly convert indigenous people, charge Christians deny; photo (M)

1999-01-25.title: Brazil's Economic Crisis Pits President Against Governors
1999-01-25.abstract: Resistance to economic reform by group of powerful state governors creates dangerous impasse in Brazil's struggle to stabilize economy; latest crisis was touched off when Minas Gerais Gov Itamar Franco set unilateral 90-day moratorium on repayment of state's estimated $13.5 billion debt to Government; other opposition governors score Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso's austerity plan and seek to renegotiate their debt (M)

1999-01-25.title: As Arab League Urges Iraqis to Obey the U.N., They Walk Out of the Meeting
1999-01-25.abstract: Iraqi delegation led by Min Mohammed Said al-Sahaf walks out of Arab League meeting, Cairo, as Arab foreign ministers demand Iraq renounce 'provocations' against neighbors and comply with United Nations resolutions before economic sanctions are lifted; al-Sahaf blames Saudi Arabia, Egypt and Syria for anti-Iraq tone of statement, news conference; warns United States tries to use Arab stand as pretext for new attacks; photo (M)

1999-01-25.title: Albright Seeks to Reassure Moscow on 'Star Wars' Plan
1999-01-25.abstract: State Sec Madeleine K Albright, traveling to Russia, holds Clinton Administration plan to develop missile defense system would not affect any Russian decision to ratify Start II treaty on reducing nuclear arsenals, which has languished in Russian Parliament since 1993 (M)

1999-01-26.title: Podujevo Journal; In a Kosovo Town, the Serbs Feel Persecuted Too
1999-01-26.abstract: Serbs in Kosovo village of Podujevo counter new reports on persecution of ethnic Albanians with accounts of intimidation by Kosovo Liberation Army; village that was half Serbian 25 years ago now has population less than one percent Serb, although government is still run by Serbs; photo; map (Podujevo Journal) (S)

1999-01-26.title: John Paul Tells Mexicans That Faith Must Overcome Corruption
1999-01-26.abstract: Pope John Paul II bids farewell to Mexico with message that faith must help society, particularly peasants and indigenous peoples, overcome corruption that permeates many institutions; calls on Mexico to root out drug trafficking and violence dividing social classes; addresses fervent crowd at Aztec Stadium before departing for St Louis, where he will meet Pres Clinton (S)

1999-01-26.title: Without Beijing Nod, Town Has Election
1999-01-26.abstract: Remote farming region in central China holds country's first direct elections for township leader, apparently without formal approval from Beijing; local party's favorite candidate won in Buyun, anyway (S)

1999-01-26.title: U.S. Admits Missile Misfire May Have Hit Iraq Civilians
1999-01-26.abstract: American jets attack several air-defense sites in northern and southern Iraq; Pentagon says at least one missile misfired, and might have killed several civilians; Iraq claims 11 civilians dead and 59 wounded; photo; map; Gen Anthony C Zinni says jets attacked after four MIG fighters flew into southern no-flight zone to try to lure Americans into range of surface-to-air missiles; regrets civilian casualties, but blames Saddam Hussein for initiating exchanges (S)

1999-01-26.title: King Hussein Selects Eldest Son, Abdullah, as Successor
1999-01-26.abstract: King Hussein of Jordan decrees that his eldest son, Prince Abdullah, is next in line for throne; photo; designation ends 51-year-old Prince Hassan's position as crown prince after 34 years; king recently issued public letter of criticism of Hassan, his brother, scholarly Oxford-educated technocrat more comfortable in blazer than in Arab dress; Prince Abdullah, 36, is son of king's English second wife, Toni Avril Gardiner or Princess Muna, whom king divorced in 1971; prince attended Sandhurst military academy in England, as well as Deerfield Academy in Illinois (S)

1999-01-27.title: Tel Aviv Journal; Battling Israel's Great Divide: A Schoolgirl Is Star
1999-01-27.abstract: Tel Aviv Journal on film by Senyora Bar-David underscoring rift in Israel between Ashkenazi Jews of Central and Eastern European descent and Sephardic Jewish immigrants from North Africa and Arab countries, who suffer discrimination; subject of film is 17-year-old Elinor Nagar, Sephardic Jew who is enrolled in 25-year-old integration program aimed at giving such teen-agers shot at social mobility through education in quality high schools; Bar-David is graduate of program; photo (M)

1999-01-27.title: King Hussein Returns to U.S. With Possible Cancer Relapse
1999-01-27.abstract: King Hussein of Jordan, hours after anointing son as new heir, returns to US for urgent medical treatment prompted by what doctors say are indications that his cancer may have recurred; doctors say King suffered fevers and blood-count abnormalities since returning from US on January 19; Jordan faces prospect that Hussein, who has had diagnoses of cancer twice in last six years, may be sicker than anyone knew (M)

1999-01-27.title: Lord Lewin, 78, British Strategist in Falklands
1999-01-27.abstract: Sir Terence Thornton Lewin, British 'seadog' who is credited with engineering his country's victory over Argentina in Falkland Islands war in 1982, dies at age 78; photo (M)

1999-01-27.title: U.N. Report: 'Confusion Reigns' On Details
1999-01-27.abstract: Excerpts from report to Security Council by UN arms inspector Richard Butler accusing Iraq's Pres Saddam Hussein and other Govt officials of actively trying to conceal past and present illegal weapons programs (M)

1999-01-27.title: At Site of Indonesia Riots, Few Signs of Life
1999-01-27.abstract: More than 40 houses are destroyed by fire and upwards of 40 Christian residents slain by enraged Muslim mob in northern Indonesia; killings follow five days of rioting, looting and arson that destroyed Christian and Muslim villages in northeast town of Ambon in Spice Islands or Moluccas (S)

1999-01-28.title: U.S. Backs a 3-Part Review of Iraq-U.N. Relations
1999-01-28.abstract: US throws support behind Canadian plan to convene three expert panels to review all aspects of Iraq's relations with UN in effort to break Security Council policy stalemate (M)

1999-01-28.title: Jordan Nervously Awaits Royal Drama's Outcome
1999-01-28.abstract: Crown Prince Abdullah, son of ailing King Hussein of Jordan, receives well-wishers for first time in Amman after Hussein named him successor in place of brother, Crown Prince Hassan; his youth and inexperience have caused anxiety over country's future, even though Hussein has expressed 'categorical confidence and trust' in son; Hussein's doctors at Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minn, confirm he is suffering from relapse of non-Hodgkins lymphoma; worries over much longer he may reign have sharpened bafflement and disbelief over his abrupt change of successors; photo (M)

1999-01-28.title: Violence Erupts as Colombia Learns What Was Lost in Quake, and What Remains
1999-01-28.abstract: Residents of Armenia, Colombia, angered by delays in assistance after one of worst earthquakes in more than century, loot supermarkets and stores and attack soldiers and policemen; extent of destruction has strained to breaking point country's capacity to deliver emergency assistance; devastation in Armenia described; photos; maps (M)

1999-01-28.title: Albright to Meet Jordan's Heir
1999-01-28.abstract: US Sec of State Madeleine K Albright will meet with Crown Prince Abdullah, son and new successor to ailing King Hussein of Jordan, in Amman on January 28; courtesy call is intended to demonstrate importance US attaches to Jordan as pivotal ally (S)

1999-01-28.title: World Briefing
1999-01-28.abstract: EUROPE    GERMANY: GREENS PRESS FOR A-PLANTS' CLOSINGS -- Undaunted by Chancellor Gerhard Schroder's decision to delay plans to abandon nuclear energy, the Green Party said it would press to close three or four nuclear power plants in four years. Mr. Schroder said on Tuesday that he had dropped a proposed ban on exporting nuclear waste for reprocessing. The ban would have been a first step to closing Germany's 19 nuclear plants.   (AP)

1999-01-29.title: 3 Days After Quake in Colombia, a Tapping in the Rubble Tells of Life
1999-01-29.abstract: Rescue workers at site of powerful earthquake in Armenia, Colombia, say they might still find people buried alive under tons of rubble; efforts to aid tens of thousands of survivors continue to encounter problems; Pres Andres Pastrana, responding to complaints that Government is doing less to help than private relief organizations, flies to area to supervise Government's response personally; most widely requsted item is not food but plastic sheeting for many homeless people to erect makeshift tents; photos; map (M)

1999-01-29.title: World Bank Cuts Off Loans to 2 Brazilian States
1999-01-29.abstract: World Bank freezes $209 million in development loans to two Brazilian states locked in economic war with central Government, Minas Gerais and Rio Grande do Sul; move is clear bid to support beleaguered Government of Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso; Itamar Franco, governor of Minas Gerais, set off crisis by suspending payments on $13.5 billion of debt to federal government for 90 days; Gov Olivo Dutra of Rio Grande do Sul is paying his state's debt into escrow account; two staet governments claim they inherited deficit-ridden administrations after elections last October (M)

1999-01-29.title: World Briefing
1999-01-29.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: STRENGTHENING UNIONS' RIGHTS -- The Labor Government, in a direct rejection of Thatcherism, announced plans to reinforce the role of unions. A proposed Employment Relations Bill would restore the statutory right of unions to recognition by companies employing more than 20 people if the workers want to be unionized.   (Reuters)

1999-01-29.title: 8 Killed in Explosion in China; Bomb Is Suspected
1999-01-29.abstract: Explosion in Yizhang, in southeastern China, kills at least 8 people and injures more than 60; officials say cause of blast has not been determined, but local journalist says there is evidence that it was intentional, and one doctor says victims had nails in their wounds, suggesting possibility of homemade nail bomb; explosion is second this month in Hunan province, site of recent worker and farmer protests over corruption, unpaid wages and taxes (M)

1999-01-29.title: Suit Says Canada Imported Tainted Blood From U.S. Inmates
1999-01-29.abstract: Canadian Government and several private companies are sued for $660 million by group of Canadians who claim they were infected with hepatitis C by tainted blood products collected from prisoners in United States; class-action suit charges Canada with neglect for failing to keep out blood plasma collected in Arkansas prisons in early 1980's after health officials in US had already determined that using such products was 'imprudent' because there was high risk of infection with hepatitis C; commercial blood broker in Montreal and Toronto laboratory are also charged in suit, which is seeking compensation for 1,000 hemophiliacs who contracted hepatitis C from 1980 to 1985; it is latest development in long and painful episode involving deaths or infections of thousands of Canadians who received tainted blood from several sources in 1980's (M)

1999-01-30.title: Gore Seeks to Protect Trade From World's Fiscal Crisis
1999-01-30.abstract: Vice Pres Al Gore, in speech in Davos, Switzerland, to world's richest industrialists and top government officials, says that world has to 'prevent the financial crisis of 1998 from becoming trade crisis of 1999'; says US will seek 'outright elimination' of huge and politically sensitive subsidies that European nations provide to farmers (M)

1999-01-30.title: U.S. Planning to Seek Further Sanctions on Libya
1999-01-30.abstract: Clinton Administration officials are seeking tougher economic sanctions against Libya one month before US deadline for Libya to hand over two suspects in bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 over Scotland in 1988; say Col Muammar el-Qaddafi has given no sign that he will accept compromise from US and Britain on trial in case; suspects, Abdel Basset Ali Megrahi and Lamen Khalifa Fhimah, were indicted in 1991 (M)

1999-01-30.title: Star-Crossed Pakistani Couple Diverted From Flight to 'Happy Ever After'
1999-01-30.abstract: Mehmood Butt and his wife, Humaira, were married in Pakistan despite opposition of her father, Punjab legislator Abbas Khokar; couple tried to flee to US, but landed in police custody in Lahore; photo (M)

1999-01-30.title: Allies Call Kosovo Rivals To Peace Talks in France
1999-01-30.abstract: US, four Western European nations and Russia, in unusual show of unity, summon warring sides in Kosovo to peace conference in France on Feb 5, and suggest that if they did not reach agreement within two weeks, air strikes would follow; photo of Sec of State Madeleine K Albright and British Foreign Sec Robin Cook at news conference in London (M)

1999-01-30.title: U.S. AIDES PUSHING PLAN TO USE G.I.'S IN A KOSOVO TRUCE
1999-01-30.abstract: Pres Clinton's top national security advisers decide that US should commit several thousand ground troops to international force in Kosovo to monitor eventual cease-fire there; Administration officials are promoting plan around Capitol Hill before discussing it with Clinton; tentative plan would involve 2,000 to 5,000 US troops in NATO force of over 20,000 ground troops; under one proposal, troops could remain in Kosovo for as long as three years; French and British have agreed to send ground troops to Kosovo as part of NATO peacekeeping effort (M)

1999-01-31.title: Hong Kong Ruling on Children May Open a Door From China
1999-01-31.abstract: Hong Kong court, in landmark decision, rules four children born in China to Hong Kong parents have right to live in enclave; asserts local laws take precedence over Chinese laws, which make it very hard to move to Hong Kong; officials expect ruling to spur flood of mainland residents to try to enter Hong Kong, both legally and illegally; pro-democracy leaders hail ruling as affirmation of judicial independence (M)

1999-01-31.title: Reporter Finds Russia Has Not Outlived Secrecy
1999-01-31.abstract: Closed-door trial of Capt Grigory Pasko, charged with treason for reporting about dumping of military waste at sea, shows there are still limits on free speech in Russia, especially when military is concerned; follows arrest arrest of Aleksandr Nikitin, retired Navy officer who researched radioactive contamination caused by Russia's northern fleet; is being held in Vladivostok, far from Moscow's media glare; Pasko, career Navy officer, was working as reporter for Navy newspaper and freelancing for Japanese television network when arrested; map; photos (M)

1999-01-31.title: Briton Issues An Ultimatum To Both Sides Over Kosovo
1999-01-31.abstract: British Foreign Sec Robin Cook warns Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic and ethnic Albanian leaders that they must show up in France to negotiate self-government for Serbia's embattled Kosovo province or face NATO bombing and other military action; meets with Milosevic and Serbian Pres Milan Milutinovic in Belgrade and ethnic Albanians, including Ibrahim Rugova and Adem Demaci in Skope, Macedonia; Serbian forces in Kosovo continue to hit ethnic Albanian fighters hard, seeking to seize as much ground as possible before talks; map; photo (M)

1999-01-31.title: Attacks on Christians a 'Blot' on India, Premier Tells Nation
1999-01-31.abstract: Indian Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee scores recent attacks on Christian places of worship and murders of Australian missionary Graham Staines and his two young sons, television speech marking 51st anniversary of assassination of Mohandas K Gandhi; speech fails to quiet charges that he moves too slowly against sectarian violence and too quickly to clear Hindu militants linked to his Hindu nationalist party; party leader, Madan Lal Khurana, resigns from Government in part over anti-Christian violence; warn militants damage image of party and India (M)

1999-01-31.title: European Diplomats Use Sticks
1999-01-31.abstract: Analysis of effort by NATO's European allies to impose peace accord in Kosovo, Serbia, with United States backing; France and Britain will take on facilitator's role US assumed in Bosnian peace talks and provide much of peacekeeping force; are eager to show Europe is now capable of taking initiative; photo of Rambouillet, France, chateau where talks will be held (M)
